title-group.article-title: Role of the WNT signalling pathway in physiological and pathological blood-brain barrier

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The blood–brain barrier (BBB) is essential for maintaining central nervous system (CNS) homeostasis and protecting neural tissue. The wingless-type MMTV integration site family (WNT) signalling pathway has emerged as a key regulator of BBB development, maintenance, and repair. Dysregulation of this pathway is implicated in BBB dysfunction associated with various neurological disorders.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We conducted a comprehensive review of recent literature integrating data from animal models, human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived BBB systems, and disease-specific mechanistic studies. The role of canonical and non-canonical WNT signalling in BBB formation, maturation, and pathological alteration was systematically analyzed.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: WNT7a/b ligands activate β-catenin-dependent signalling to drive cerebral angiogenesis and BBB differentiation, with G protein‑coupled receptor 124 (GPR124), Reversion‑inducing cysteine‑rich protein with Kazal motifs (RECK), and SRY‑related HMG‑box transcription factor 17 (Sox17) identified as critical co-regulators. In the mature BBB, WNT activity is suppressed epigenetically to maintain barrier stability. In diseases such as ischaemic stroke, Alzheimer’s disease, multiple sclerosis, and glioblastoma, WNT signalling is disrupted, leading to BBB breakdown. Pharmacological activation of WNT/β-catenin signalling (e.g. lithium, Glycogen synthase kinase 3β (GSK-3β) inhibitors and engineered WNT ligands) restores BBB integrity in preclinical models. Additionally, modulation of WNT signalling can enhance drug delivery across the BBB, offering therapeutic advantages in brain tumours and neurodegenerative diseases.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: WNT signalling is a central molecular axis governing BBB integrity under both physiological and pathological conditions. Targeted modulation of this pathway represents a promising therapeutic strategy for restoring BBB function and improving CNS drug delivery. Further mechanistic and translational studies are warranted to advance clinical applications.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) is a highly specialized neurovascular interface that strictly regulates molecular exchange between the circulation and central nervous system (CNS) parenchyma. Unlike peripheral vasculature, CNS capillaries form continuous tight junctions (TJs) between endothelial cells, creating a physical barrier reinforced by pericytes, astrocytic endfeet, and basement membrane components [1]. This structural organization enables precise control of paracellular permeability while facilitating essential transport processes: low density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1-mediated clearance of β-amyloid (Aβ) opposes receptor for advanced glycation end products-mediated influx to maintain cerebral proteostasis; polarized aquaporin-4 (AQP4) channels on astrocytic endfeet drive convective glymphatic exchange between cerebrospinal and interstitial fluids [2,3]; and endothelial Na+/K+-ATPase activity maintains ionic gradients essential for neurotransmitter homeostasis and metabolic waste removal [4]. Beyond barrier function, the BBB exhibits metabolic enzyme activity and serves as an immunological sentinel, regulating immune cell surveillance and cytokine trafficking [5,6]. The successful formation and maintenance of the BBB ensure the optimal chemical composition of the neuronal microenvironment. Importantly, the BBB is not an absolute impermeable barrier; instead, it functions as a dynamic, selectively permeable interface. A wide range of neurological pathologies are characterized by disease-specific structural and functional remodelling of the BBB, resulting in barrier dysfunction that actively contributes to disease progression. Consequently, preserving or restoring BBB integrity is essential for the treatment and management of these pathological conditions [7,8].

The wingless-type MMTV integration site family (WNT) signalling pathway is an evolutionarily conserved and complex signalling cascade that can be classified into 2 categories: canonical (β-catenin-dependent) and noncanonical (β-catenin-independent) [9]. The classic β-catenin-dependent pathway has a high degree of interaction with the establishment of barrier characteristics of brain microvascular endothelial cells, the regulation of tight junction function, and the maintenance of neurovascular unit homeostasis. Meanwhile, abnormal activation or inhibition of the pathway will directly mediate the structural disruption and functional disorder of the BBB, and participate in the occurrence and development of neurological diseases [10]. The human genome, similar to most mammals, contains 19 WNT genes grouped into 12 conserved subfamilies [9,11]. In the absence of ligand, β-catenin undergoes continuous proteasomal degradation mediated by the cytoplasmic destruction complex (Axis inhibitor 2 (Axin2), Glycogen synthase kinase 3β (GSK-3β), Casein Kinase 1 (CK1)). Upon WNT binding to Frizzled (FZD) receptors and LDL receptor-related protein 5/6 (LRP5/6) co-receptors, this degradation is inhibited, enabling β-catenin accumulation, nuclear translocation, and T-cell factor/lymphoid enhancer factor-mediated transcription of genes essential for tight junction formation, endothelial differentiation, and neurovascular unit integrity [12]. The noncanonical WNT signalling pathway regulates cell polarity and directional movement through interactions between FZD receptors and various coreceptor partners, activating non-β-catenin-dependent effectors that regulate cellular activity [13,14]. Pathologically, cerebrovascular WNT/β-catenin signalling operates within a narrow homeostatic window. Pathway insufficiency accelerates β-catenin degradation, downregulating tight junction proteins and increasing paracellular permeability, whereas excessive activation disrupts vascular patterning and impairs barrier maturation. The Phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase/GSK3β axis serves as a critical regulatory node, with GSK3β-mediated β-catenin phosphorylation marking it for ubiquitination and proteasomal turnover [15,16]. Consequently, targeted modulation of this pathway-whether through GSK3β inhibitors to stabilize β-catenin or pathway-specific agonists to restore transcriptional activity-represents a fundamental strategy for maintaining or repairing BBB integrity under pathological conditions. To this end, this narrative review was conducted through a literature search of PubMed, covering publications from January 2008 to January 2026. The following search terms were applied in combination: ‘WNT signalling’, ‘WNT/β-catenin signalling pathway’, ‘blood-brain barrier’, ‘BBB’, ‘cerebrovascular’, ‘neurological disease’, ‘ischaemic stroke’, ‘Alzheimer’s disease’, ‘multiple sclerosis’ and ‘glioblastoma’ and so on. Studies were selected based on their relevance to WNT signalling in BBB biology, including data from animal models, human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived BBB systems, and disease-specific mechanistic studies. Inclusion criteria were: (1) peer-reviewed original research articles, reviews, or meta-analyses published in English; (2) studies addressing WNT pathway components in the context of BBB biology, development, maintenance, or pathological disruption; (3) studies utilizing in vivo animal models, human iPSC-derived BBB systems, or clinical/translational datasets. Exclusion criteria were: (1) conference abstracts, editorials, and non-peer-reviewed sources; (2) studies not directly addressing WNT signalling or BBB function; and (3) duplicate publications. The role of canonical and non-canonical WNT signalling in BBB formation, maturation, and pathological alteration was comprehensively analyzed.

sec.title: WNT pathway in BBB development
sec.p: The roles of WNT7a and WNT7b in CNS vascular development were comprehensively characterized in 2008. These ligands act through β-catenin activation and serve as pivotal regulators of cerebrovascular formation [17]. Experimental evidence indicates that WNT7a/WNT7b signalling is indispensable for endothelial assembly and differentiation within defined CNS regions. Their deletion results in severe vascular malformations, including abnormal morphology and compromised functionality, underscoring the essential contribution of WNT signalling to CNS vascular integrity and the maintenance of the BBB [18].

The adhesion G protein‑coupled receptor 124 (GPR124) and the glycosylphosphatidylinositol-anchored protein Reversion‑inducing cysteine‑rich protein with Kazal motifs (RECK) function as critical co-regulators of this signalling cascade [19,20]. Mechanistically, RECK directly interacts with WNT7, stabilizing its active monomeric form and enhancing its binding to FZD receptors, thereby amplifying canonical WNT/β-catenin activity. GPR124 exerts its modulatory effects primarily via its extracellular domain (ECD), facilitating RECK-mediated WNT7 stabilization through ECD-dependent interactions. Notably, this mechanism is independent of its transmembrane and intracellular domains [21]. While genetic loss-of-function studies clearly establish WNT7a/WNT7b as indispensable for CNS angiogenesis, the relative contributions of ligand abundance versus receptor-complex composition (GPR124, RECK, and FZD isoforms) remain incompletely resolved. Notably, regional differences in vascular phenotypes indicate that BBB specification may depend less on absolute WNT signal strength than on context-dependent assembly of the WNT receptor complex. A closely related receptor, G protein-coupled receptor 126 (GPR126), has recently been identified as a downstream effector of WNT/β-catenin signalling. GPR126 promotes endothelial cell migration, a prerequisite for angiogenesis, by regulating interactions with low-density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 1 and β1 integrin, thereby maintaining a functional balance between β1 integrin activation and recycling [22]. Figure 1 schematically illustrates the major components and structural organization of the BBB.

fig.label: Figure 1.

caption.p: The blood-brain barrier (BBB) is a complex structure formed through the precise collaboration of multiple cell types. It is composed of brain capillary endothelial cells, the basement membrane, astrocytic end-feet, and pericytes. Tight junction proteins (including Claudin-5, Occludin, ZO-1, etc.) are densely distributed between adjacent endothelial cells, which strictly limit paracellular permeability and maintain the integrity and homeostasis of the BBB. This multicellular complex functions to isolate and block toxins, maintain homeostasis of the internal environment, and facilitate selective transport. The formation, maintenance, and function of the BBB are closely linked to the WNT signalling pathway. Receptors for WNT signalling, such as FZD and LRP6, are expressed on cerebrovascular endothelial cells. Upon ligand binding and receptor activation, the intracellular β-catenin pathway is activated.

fig.alt-text: Detailed diagram of brain anatomy, highlighting progenitor cells, blood-brain barrier components, tight junction proteins, and a signaling pathway.
fig.long-desc: This multi-panel diagram illustrates the brain’s anatomy and its cellular interactions. The left panel shows a labeled image of the brain, while adjacent sections depict endothelial progenitor cells, mesodermal progenitor cells, and neural stem cells. The central part details the blood-brain barrier, including capillary endothelial cells, astrocytes, pericytes, and ions such as Ca²? and Na?. An inset highlights tight junction proteins (ZO-1, Claudin-5, Occludin). Another inset describes the Norrin/Wnt signaling pathway, showcasing interactions between various proteins and the activation of beta-catenin.

sec.p: Norrin provides an atypical route to β-catenin activation, engaging FZD4 via the dedicated co-receptor Tspan12 rather than the GPR124/RECK complex. In specific CNS regions, notably the cerebellum, glial-derived WNT7a signalling demonstrates functional redundancy and complementarity with Norrin-mediated activation [23,24]. In ventral regions and the cerebellum, Norrin and WNT7 exhibit partial functional overlap yet generate non-identical vascular phenotypes, suggesting ligand-specific outputs. This spatial specificity extends to the dorsoventral axis: while WNT7a/b and Norrin dominate ventral patterning, dorsal vasculature relies on distinct ligands (WNT1, WNT3/3a and WNT4) [20,25]. Comparative studies of Norrin- and WNT7-driven signalling revealed partial redundancy yet non-identical outcomes, raising the unresolved question of whether these ligands converge on identical transcriptional programs or instead generate region-specific BBB phenotypes through subtle differences in β-catenin dynamics. Although multiple WNT ligands are expressed in spatially distinct CNS regions, direct comparative analyses of their endothelial target genes are lacking, limiting our understanding of whether BBB development follows a unified WNT code or multiple ligand-specific programs.

In 2012, Stephen J. Tam et al. proposed a developmental transcriptomic approach to elucidate the molecular determinants of BBB formation across embryonic, neonatal, and adult stages [26]. Their analyses revealed enrichment of WNT/β-catenin target genes and previously known BBB-associated components. Notably, the death receptors, death receptor 6 and tumour necrosis factor receptor orphan Y, were markedly enriched in the CNS vasculature of zebrafish and murine embryos. Functionally, these receptors act not only as essential modulators of CNS-specific angiogenesis but also as downstream transcriptional targets of WNT/β-catenin signalling, highlighting the crucial role of WNT/β-catenin signalling in BBB regulation via control of death receptor expression and vascular remodelling [26].

In 2016, Fibroblast growth factor-binding protein 1 (Fgfbp1) (a matricellular protein that organizes collagen IV deposition) was identified as a novel downstream target of classical WNT signalling [27]. Once vascular networks are established, WNT signalling transitions from promoting cellular motility to enforcing structural stabilization, which induces Fgfbp1 to support endothelial-pericyte adhesion, and upregulates SRY‑related HMG‑box transcription factor 17 (Sox17) to form a self-reinforcing transcriptional circuit that sustains β-catenin activity during maturation [28]. Together, the opposing effects of Sox17-mediated positive feedback and retinoic acid-dependent restraint highlight that BBB development requires precise temporal control of WNT signalling; however, how this balance is established in vivo and whether it differs between brain regions remains unresolved.

Further work by Stephanie Bonney et al. (2016) demonstrated that retinoic acid functions as an upstream modulator of WNT signalling, suppressing pathway inhibitors while simultaneously acting as a regulatory brake to prevent excessive WNT activation and Sox17 overexpression. This dual inhibition maintains homeostatic balance within endothelial signalling networks [29]. Complementing these transcriptional programs, noncanonical WNT signalling regulates TJ stability through cytoskeletal mechanisms. The partitioning-defective/atypical protein kinase C complex colocalizes with TJs and governs their stability by controlling apicobasal polarity [30], with WNT5a enhancing TJ integrity via this pathway independent of β-catenin transcription. Thus, canonical and noncanonical pathways operate in parallel during BBB maturation, integrating transcriptional amplification with cytoskeletal polarization to establish barrier integrity.

sec.title: Maintenance role of the WNT pathway in the mature BBB
sec.p: During the later stages of BBB maturation, the activity of the WNT/β-catenin signalling pathway progressively declines, a change that corresponds with the establishment of a stable, fully functional barrier [31]. In the late embryonic cerebral cortex, the stabilization of vascular networks requires precise modulation of WNT signalling levels: excessive activation of the pathway induces vascular regression, whereas its inhibition markedly prevents this process. In the physiological environment, radial glial progenitors communicate with developing endothelial cells to fine-tune WNT signalling and thereby ensure vascular stabilization. The depletion of these progenitors leads to pronounced vascular degeneration, which is accompanied by aberrant WNT/β-catenin activation in endothelial cells [32].

Once the BBB achieves full maturity, WNT signalling activity remains basally low [33]. Transcriptomic analyses reveal that while upstream receptors (FZD4/6, LRP5) remain expressed, downstream targets (Axin2, lymphoid enhancer-binding factor 1 (LEF1)) are epigenetically silenced via histone H3 lysine 27 trimethylation (H3K27me3) enrichment and reduced H3K4me3/histone H3 lysine 9 acetylation at promoters [9]. This repression is mediated by the enrichment of repressive histone modification H3K27me3 at promoter regions together with reduced levels of active histone marks H3K4me3 and H3K9ac. Such epigenetic remodelling is essential for maintaining BBB homeostasis—limiting excessive WNT activity to preserve selective permeability and protect the CNS against circulating toxins [34]. Although multiple studies reported the reduced WNT/β-catenin activity in the mature BBB, experimental manipulation revealed that both insufficient and excessive pathway activation can destabilize vascular integrity, indicating a narrow homeostatic window that has not yet been quantitatively defined. These epigenetic findings demonstrate that WNT signalling competence is retained in adult endothelial cells but actively suppressed; however, whether this repression is uniform across vascular segments or dynamically reversible during injury remains unclear.

For decades, astrocytes have been recognized as key contributors to the trophic maintenance of BBB physiology through the secretion of regulatory factors [35,36]. Transcriptomic analyses reveal that while upstream receptors (FZD4/6, LRP5) remain expressed, downstream targets (Axin2, LEF1) are epigenetically silenced via H3K27me3 enrichment and reduced H3K4me3/H3K9ac at promoters. At the cellular level, astrocytic endfeet displayed prominent swelling and mislocalization of AQP4, the principal water channel regulating perivascular fluid exchange. These findings demonstrate that astrocyte-derived WNT growth factors are indispensable for maintaining both structural integrity and physiological function of the mature BBB [37]. Notably, despite profound BBB leakage, the absence of astrocyte-derived WNT did not overtly disrupt endothelial junctional ultrastructure, implying that astrocytic WNT primarily regulates functional polarization and water homeostasis rather than junction assembly per se—an important distinction that warrants further investigation.

sec.title: Role of the WNT pathway in the establishment of the BBB model
sec.p: BBB models are central to the evaluation of CNS drug penetration; however, in vivo animal models continue to represent the most physiologically integrative framework for assessing brain permeability, despite well-recognized limitations in scalability and translational fidelity. Although in vivo animal models remain the benchmark for BBB studies, they are limited by high cost, low throughput, and interspecies variability. Access to primary human brain endothelial cells is scarce, and many immortalized human endothelial lines fail to form sufficiently tight barriers in vitro. Consequently, human-induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)–derived BBB models have emerged as a scalable, reproducible, and human-relevant alternative [38]. While iPSC-derived BBB models recapitulate many WNT-dependent transcriptional features of CNS endothelium, their reliance on sustained WNT activation contrasts with the low-WNT state observed in the mature BBB in vivo, raising questions about how faithfully these models represent adult barrier physiology.

One robust approach employs umbilical cord blood–derived haematopoietic stem cells, which are differentiated into endothelial cells (ECs) and cocultured with pericytes to induce BBB characteristics [39]. This induction is partially mediated by WNT/β-catenin signalling. The resulting brain-like endothelial cells (BLECs) express canonical TJ proteins and transporters characteristic of brain endothelium and maintain BBB properties for at least 20 days. Importantly, this system shows high reproducibility across donors and laboratories and reliably predicts compound penetration into the human CNS [40,41].

Canonical WNT activation also plays an instructive role in human pluripotent stem cell (hPSC)–derived endothelial progenitors, driving acquisition of BBB-specific traits. WNT stimulation induces increased Glucose transporter 1 (GLUT-1) and claudin-5 expression, repression of plasmalemma vesicle-associated protein (PLVAP), and reduced paracellular permeability. Transcriptomic analyses reveal enrichment of CNS-endothelial gene signatures, including LEF1, Adenomatosis polyposis coli down-regulated 1 (APCDD1), and Zic family member 3 (ZIC3), closely resembling native brain endothelial cells [42]. These findings establish WNT signalling as a key determinant of endothelial specialization towards a BBB phenotype. Notably, pericyte-induced BBB properties are only partially WNT-dependent, indicating that additional signalling pathways converge with WNT to establish full barrier competence—an interaction that remains incompletely defined in current models.

Using optimized differentiation protocols, hPSCs can be efficiently specified into brain microvascular endothelial cells (BMECs) via stepwise signalling modulation. This process begins with canonical WNT-driven mesoderm induction, followed by endothelial commitment and subsequent retinoic acid–mediated maturation, yielding cells with organized TJs, polarized transporter activity, and responsiveness to astrocyte-derived cues [43–45]. Notably, hPSC-derived BMECs exhibit robust barrier function, with transendothelial electrical resistance (TEER) values exceeding 3000 Ω·cm2, consistent with in vivo BBB properties [45]. Although high TEER values are mainly interpreted as functional equivalence to the in vivo BBB, differences in transcytosis rates, inflammatory responsiveness, and long-term stability suggest that WNT-driven in vitro models may preferentially capture developmental rather than fully mature endothelial states.

Consistent with developmental paradigms, sustained BBB characteristics can also be achieved in immortalized mouse and human brain endothelial cell lines through WNT3a supplementation, pharmacological stabilization of β-catenin, or expression of constitutively active β-catenin—independent of astrocyte coculture. These approaches preserve BBB marker expression, high TEER, and low permeability across multiple passages and laboratories [46]. Collectively, these studies underscore WNT/β-catenin signalling as a central axis for establishing and maintaining functional in vitro BBB models [47]. Despite extensive identification of β-catenin-regulated genes, it remains unclear which subset is strictly required for BBB identity versus those that reflect broader endothelial differentiation programs, highlighting a key gap between transcriptomic profiling and functional necessity.

Mechanistic support for this concept comes from comparative transcriptomic analyses of brain endothelial cells from wild-type and β-catenin–deficient mice, which identified critical BBB-regulatory genes—including forkhead box F2, forkhead box L2, forkhead box Q1, LEF1, peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor delta, zinc finger protein 551, and zinc finger protein Zic family member 3. Notably, Forkhead box L2 and zinc finger protein Zic family member 3 can induce BBB differentiation markers in non-CNS endothelial cells, highlighting their instructive roles downstream of WNT signalling [48]. Together, these molecular insights provide a rational framework for the engineering and optimization of human BBB models, thereby accelerating translational research and therapeutic development for neurological diseases [49].

sec.title: Role of the WNT signalling pathway in BBB-related diseases
sec.p: As outlined above, the WNT signalling pathway is indispensable for BBB development, maturation, and maintenance, as well as for the establishment of in vitro BBB models. Dysregulation of WNT signalling is increasingly recognized as a common mechanistic denominator underlying BBB dysfunction in a wide range of neurological disorders. In this section, we summarize current evidence linking altered WNT signalling to BBB disruption in stroke, neurodegenerative diseases, and other brain-related pathologies, with particular emphasis on endothelial WNT/β-catenin signalling.

sec.title: Stroke and the WNT signalling pathway
sec.p: Stroke is the third leading cause of death worldwide, according to the Global Burden of Disease Study [50]. Clinically, stroke is classified into ischaemic and haemorrhagic subtypes, with ischaemic stroke accounting for the majority of cases [51]. BBB breakdown in ischaemic stroke promotes vasogenic edoema and haemorrhagic transformation, marked by disassembly of endothelial TJs [52]. BBB TJs are composed of transmembrane proteins—including claudins, occludin, tricellulin, and junctional adhesion molecules—which are anchored to the actin cytoskeleton via zonula occludens (ZO) proteins, thereby ensuring barrier integrity [53]. Notably, while insufficient WNT signalling exacerbates BBB disruption in ischaemic stroke, emerging evidence suggests that WNT overexpression or excessive activation may also perturb BBB homeostasis, highlighting the need for a balanced regulatory state of the pathway.

Genetic studies have revealed a critical role for endothelial GPR124-dependent WNT signalling in BBB stability under pathological conditions. While endothelial-specific deletion of GPR124 does not compromise BBB integrity under physiological conditions, its loss markedly exacerbates haemorrhage in disease contexts such as ischaemic stroke and glioblastoma, accompanied by attenuated canonical WNT/β-catenin signalling in cerebral vessels [54]. Conversely, targeted WNT overexpression in cerebral endothelial cells has been shown to enhance TJ protein expression and reduce BBB leakage in acute ischaemic stroke models, confirming the protective potential of appropriate WNT activation. Notably, endothelial-specific activation of β-catenin rescues the haemorrhagic phenotype in GPR124-deficient mice, underscoring the central role of canonical WNT signalling in vascular protection. However, unregulated overexpression of β-catenin in endothelial cells may lead to abnormal vascular proliferation and altered TJ assembly, suggesting that precise modulation of WNT/β-catenin activity is essential. Supporting this, endothelial-specific gain-of-function studies show that early and sustained β-catenin signalling prevents correct endothelial cell differentiation, upregulates the Notch ligand delta-like canonical Notch ligand 4 (Dll4), causing defective vessel branching, loss of venous identity, and embryonic lethality, demonstrating that β-catenin hyperactivation disrupts vascular patterning as severely as its deficiency [55,56]. Platelet-derived growth factor subunit B (PDGFB) has been identified as a downstream effector of the GPR124–WNT axis, regulating endothelial TJs, pericyte coverage, and extracellular matrix remodelling following stroke [55].

Pharmacological modulation of WNT signalling has further highlighted its therapeutic potential. Lithium [57] is a well-established modulator of the WNT pathway through inhibition of GSK-3β [58]. In ischaemic stroke models, lithium confers BBB protection via activation of canonical WNT/β-catenin signalling in a GPR124-dependent manner [59]. However, sustained over-inhibition of GSK-3β may trigger ectopic β-catenin transcriptional activity and paradoxical disruption of barrier homeostasis, emphasizing the importance of dose titration in translational strategies targeting this pathway. Although canonical and non-canonical WNT pathways interact antagonistically during BBB development, their relative contributions during ischaemic injury remain incompletely understood [60]. Under oxygen–glucose deprivation (OGD), both pathways are downregulated in brain endothelial cells. In ischaemia–reperfusion models, reactivation of canonical WNT signalling normalizes non-canonical pathway activity without compromising the protective effects of β-catenin on BBB integrity [61].

Multiple pharmacological agents have been shown to preserve BBB function after ischaemic injury through WNT pathway activation. In human cerebral microvascular endothelial cells, exogenous WNT stimulation enhances BBB structure and function more effectively than endogenous signalling alone [62]. Curcuma saponin A activates both WNT5a and WNT/β-catenin pathways, promoting angiogenesis, vascular maturation, and BBB preservation following ischemia–reperfusion injury [63]. Similarly, JLX001, a dihydrochloride derivative of cyclovirobuxine D, alleviates BBB disruption and improves neurological recovery in middle cerebral artery occlusion/reperfusion (Middle cerebral artery occlusion/reperfusion) models by activating WNT/β-catenin signalling [64]. The ginkgolide B analog XQ-1H also protects BBB integrity after cerebral ischemia–reperfusion injury by activating the WNT/GSK-3β/β-catenin axis and upregulating TJ proteins [65].

Emerging evidence highlights microRNAs (miRNAs) as important modulators of WNT-mediated BBB regulation [66,67]. In neurological disorders associated with BBB disruption, miR-27a-3p is consistently downregulated. Endothelial miR-27a-3p enhances expression of the TJ proteins claudin-5 and occludin by suppressing GSK-3β and activating WNT/β-catenin signalling [68]. Likewise, miR-374, which is reduced following ischemia–reperfusion, targets WNT5a; its supplementation restores BBB integrity and confers neuroprotection [69]. The regulation of WNT signalling by miRNAs is inherently bidirectional: while many injury-associated miRNAs suppress WNT activity and thereby weaken the BBB, others can relieve endogenous pathway inhibitors, resulting in excessive β-catenin accumulation. The net effect on barrier integrity thus depends on the balance between pro- and anti-WNT miRNAs in the endothelial cell, a consideration that should guide future miRNA-based therapeutic design.

Proteasome dysfunction also intersects with WNT signalling in ischaemic stroke. Although broad proteasome inhibitors reduce infarct volume and neuroinflammation [70,71], their clinical utility is limited by off-target effects. Selective inhibition of the immunoproteasome subunit low molecular mass protein 2 attenuates neuroinflammation and protects against ischaemic-hypoxic BBB injury through activation of the canonical WNT pathway, while over-inhibition of proteasomal activity can lead to β-catenin accumulation and WNT overactivation, thereby disrupting BBB homeostasis [53,72].

Astrocytes play a complementary role in BBB repair following stroke. Astrocytic Na+/H+ exchanger isoform 1 (NHE1) is activated during ischemia, contributing to astrocyte swelling and BBB disruption. Genetic deletion of astrocytic NHE1 limits BBB damage and improves neurological outcomes [73,74]. Transcriptomic analyses revealed marked upregulation of WNT7a in NHE1-deficient astrocytes. Astrocyte-derived WNT7a activates endothelial WNT/β-catenin signalling in peri-ischaemic vessels, and pharmacological inhibition of this pathway abolishes the reparative effects, demonstrating a direct astrocyte-to-endothelium WNT-dependent repair mechanism [19]. However, excessive astrocytic WNT7a overexpression induced by sustained NHE1 inhibition or other pathological stimuli may lead to endothelial WNT overactivation, resulting in abnormal vascular proliferation and impaired BBB function. This interpretation is consistent with the established biology of WNT7a/7b in CNS angiogenesis: although these ligands are essential for the formation and BBB-specific differentiation of CNS vessels, their effects are strictly context- and dose-dependent, and deviations from the physiological signalling range disrupt rather than reinforce vascular barrier properties [75,76].

Cell-based therapies have also been explored for BBB repair, with a focus on achieving balanced WNT activation rather than overexpression. Oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs) support BBB integrity during development, and OPCs transplantation protects the BBB in acute ischaemic stroke by activating WNT/β-catenin signalling [77]. However, to mitigate safety concerns associated with cell transplantation, conditioned media approaches have been investigated. Notably, BBB stabilization induced by endothelial cell secretomes involves fine-tuned, rather than excessive, WNT activation, reflected by moderate reductions in active β-catenin levels [78].

BBB disruption also contributes to complications of stroke therapy. Tissue plasminogen activator (tPA) remains the only Food and Drug Administration-approved treatment for ischaemic stroke, but its use is limited by the risk of haemorrhagic transformation (HT) [79]. Pharmacological inhibition of GSK-3β (e.g. an inhibitor of GSK-3β such as TWS119, IM-12) reduces tPA-induced HT and BBB breakdown by activating WNT/β-catenin signalling [80,81]. Beyond ischaemic stroke, BBB disruption is a major contributor to early brain injury following subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH) [82]. The WNT activator HLY78 enhances LDL receptor-related protein 6 (LRP6) phosphorylation, stabilizes β-catenin, upregulates TJ proteins, and preserves BBB integrity after SAH [83,84]. Similarly, in traumatic brain injury (TBI), intranasal delivery of recombinant fibroblast growth factor 20 promotes angiogenesis, reduces vascular leakage, and accelerates recovery via activation of WNT/β-catenin signalling [85,86].

sec.title: Neurodegenerative diseases and the WNT signalling pathway
sec.p: Notably, BBB breakdown is increasingly recognized as an early and progressive feature of neurodegenerative diseases. In Alzheimer’s disease (AD), post-mortem analyses and advanced neuroimaging studies have demonstrated BBB disruption, particularly in the hippocampus, even at prodromal stages [87,88]. In amyloid precursor protein/presenilin 1 transgenic mice, cerebrovascular endothelial damage and BBB dysfunction emerge early and progressively worsen. Amyloid-β oligomers suppress endothelial WNT/β-catenin signalling, while targeting LRP6 restores BBB function and mitigates amyloid-induced vascular injury [89]. The relationship between WNT signalling and BBB integrity in AD is, however, not unidirectional. Evidence from adult mouse models demonstrates that endothelial β-catenin signalling is required for ongoing maintenance of the adult BBB: its loss in mature cerebral endothelial cells leads to spontaneous barrier failure, neuroinflammation, and haemorrhagic transformation [90,91]. In the context of AD, where neuroinflammatory signals are chronically elevated, there is a theoretical risk that inflammatory stimuli could simultaneously suppress endothelial WNT signalling while aberrantly activating it in reactive glia, creating spatially heterogeneous dysregulation within the neurovascular unit. Therapies aimed at restoring WNT/β-catenin activity in AD must therefore account for this cell-type-specific complexity.

In amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), iPSC-derived BMEC-like cells from patients carrying TAR DNA-binding protein 43 mutations exhibit increased permeability, elevated expression of adhesion molecules (intercellular adhesion molecule 1 (ICAM 1), vascular cell adhesion molecule 1 (VCAM 1)), and impaired WNT/β-catenin signalling. Restoration of pathway activity improves barrier function and reduces inflammatory phenotypes [92].

Progranulin (PGRN), encoded by granulin (GRN), is implicated in frontotemporal lobar degeneration (FTLD). Although overt cerebrovascular lesions are uncommon, microvascular degeneration and BBB disruption correlate with disease severity. PGRN-deficient mice display heightened vulnerability to ischaemic injury and developmental abnormalities in BBB ultrastructure, underscoring a role for WNT-associated vascular mechanisms in FTLD pathogenesis [93,94].

Multiple sclerosis (MS) is characterized by early BBB breakdown and immune cell infiltration [95]. hiPSC-derived BBB models from MS patients exhibit inflammatory activation and impaired barrier function, both of which can be rescued by activating WNT/β-catenin signalling [39]. Aberrant WNT signalling in OPCs has also been implicated in MS lesions, where excessive OPCs accumulation around blood vessels disrupts BBB integrity via secretion of WIF1, a WNT inhibitor that interferes with endothelial WNT signalling [96]. Therefore, the directionality of the strategies aimed at restoring endothelial WNT signalling must be designed to avoid simultaneously amplifying aberrant paracrine WNT activity in non-endothelial compartments. Furthermore, genome-wide association studies of treatment response to natalizumab have identified LRP6 and Growth Factor Receptor-Bound Protein 2 as key genes, linking WNT receptor signalling to therapeutic efficacy in MS [97,98].

sec.title: Other brain-related diseases and the WNT signalling pathway
sec.p: Glioblastoma is among the most vascularized and aggressive brain tumours. Although anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) therapies have failed clinically [99], emerging evidence implicates WNT/β-catenin signalling in tumour vascular normalization. In a doxycycline-inducible glioma mouse model, activation of WNT/β-catenin signalling reduced tumour growth, decreased vascular density, and promoted vessel normalization through PDGFB-mediated recruitment of vascular mural cells, thereby enhancing vascular barrier function [100,101]. Importantly, the effect of WNT activation on tumour vasculature is critically dependent on the level and duration of pathway activation. Excessive β-catenin signalling in the endothelium upregulates Dll4 and promotes aberrant angiogenic sprouting rather than vessel normalization [56,101]. These findings underscore that the therapeutic application of WNT activators in glioblastoma requires precise control of pathway intensity to achieve normalization rather than exacerbation of tumour vascular pathology. These findings suggest that augmenting WNT signalling may represent a novel strategy to suppress pathological angiogenesis in glioblastoma.

Postoperative cognitive decline (POCD) exemplifies pharmacological WNT suppression. The anesthetic sevoflurane disrupts BBB integrity by downregulating Annexin A1, a WNT/β-catenin target gene critical for endothelial membrane stability and anti-inflammatory signalling [102].

Finally, sepsis-associated encephalopathy is characterized by rapid BBB breakdown. Endotoxemia activates nuclear factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB) signalling, and the NF-κB p65 subunit directly interacts with β-catenin, suppressing β-catenin-dependent transcription. Reactivation of WNT/β-catenin signalling restores expression of ZO-1 and major facilitator superfamily domain-containing protein 2 A (Mfsd2a), thereby reducing both paracellular and transcellular BBB permeability [15]. In this context, however, the risk of WNT/β-catenin overactivation deserves consideration: GSK-3β inhibition, the most common pharmacological strategy for boosting β-catenin signalling, also exerts broad immunomodulatory effects and may impair the innate immune response to pathogens when applied systemically in the setting of active infection. The development of endothelial-targeted WNT activators that restore barrier function without systemic immunosuppression, therefore, represents an important direction for future research. Taken together, the evidence across these diverse pathological contexts converges on the principle that WNT/β-catenin signalling must be maintained within a physiological range: deviation in either direction–hypoactivation or hyperactivation–can culminate in barrier dysfunction through distinct but often overlapping molecular mechanisms.

sec.title: Therapeutic strategies targeting the WNT pathway
sec.p: WNT signalling operates as a molecular rheostat in BBB endothelial cells, dynamically balancing barrier tightness against transcytotic permeability rather than functioning as a binary switch. This duality establishes a therapeutic framework wherein pathway modulation can be tailored to either reinforce barrier integrity (e.g. in stroke or neurodegeneration) or transiently enhance drug delivery (e.g. for brain tumours), depending on disease context and treatment timing [103,104].

Inhibiting Mfsd2a has the potential to increase the efficiency of active transport across the BBB. Ju et al. developed transcytosis-targeting peptide-modified tunicamycin-loaded nanoparticles and discovered that by reversibly inhibiting Mfsd2a, the drug could be delivered more effectively to malignant brain metastatic tumours. Furthermore, inhibiting the WNT signalling pathway can promote drug delivery to the brain by downregulating Mfsd2a [105]. In a separate study, Xie et al. combined the WNT inhibitor LGK974 with chemotherapeutic agents, either temozolomide or paclitaxel, and they reported that Mfsd2a deletion and the subsequent upregulation of plasmalemma vesicle-associated protein (PLVAP) improved drug delivery and chemotherapy outcomes in glioma [106]. Tong et al. constructed nanowogonin modified with a VCAM-1-targeting peptide ligand and reported that the delivery of this nanoparticulate drug was significantly improved in brain metastases, which presented Mfsd2a deficiency and increased vesicular transport [107]. Furthermore, Mu et al. designed codelivery nanoparticles targeting ICAM-1 and loaded them with nitazoxanide and ibrutinib. This approach led to transient BBB opening and improved drug transport [108]. Importantly, these nanoparticle-based strategies highlight a critical paradigm shift: transient and endothelial-restricted inhibition of WNT signalling can be exploited to selectively suppress Mfsd2a-mediated transcytosis barriers without inducing global BBB breakdown. This suggests that the therapeutic window of WNT modulation is defined not only by pathway intensity but also by duration, cellular specificity, and vascular segment targeting. These findings support a model in which pathological BBB opening can be mechanistically distinguished from controlled, WNT-dependent permeability enhancement, emphasizing that adverse outcomes arise from uncontrolled pathway disruption rather than from precisely engineered WNT modulation. In summary, these WNT-inhibiting nanoparticles targeting VCAM-1 and ICAM-1 did not induce edoema, cognitive impairment, or systemic toxicity in normal mice, indicating the safety of this targeted WNT inhibition delivery strategy. Additional research has demonstrated that the GSK-3β inhibitor TWS119 protects the BBB via early WNT pathway activation; this mechanism increases the protein levels of β-catenin and ZO-1 and concurrently decreases β-catenin phosphorylation [109]. In addition to the WNT pathway, two other embryonic signalling pathways, the Notch pathway and the Sonic hedgehog (Shh) pathway, play central roles in the CNS [110]. Together with WNT-inhibitory delivery platforms, GSK-3β inhibition illustrates the bidirectional therapeutic potential of the WNT pathway, whereby early activation stabilizes TJ architecture, while later or localized suppression facilitates molecular transport across the BBB. Figure 2 demonstrates the crosstalk between Notch and Shh pathways and WNT signalling, and how this interplay regulates key BBB phenotypes and their manifestations. Orchestrating the pathways that crosstalk with WNT can indirectly potentiate or suppress WNT signalling, resulting in a therapeutic outcome. Rather than functioning in isolation, WNT signalling emerges as a nodal integrator in a broader endothelial signalling network, where crosstalk with Notch and Shh pathways enables fine-grained control of BBB phenotypes. Therapeutically targeting these interactions may therefore achieve more precise outcomes than direct WNT manipulation alone. Table 1 lists therapeutic strategies that exert direct or indirect effects on the WNT pathway in the context of neurological diseases.

fig.label: Figure 2.

caption.p: Developmental signalling pathway crosstalk in BBB. The WNT pathway is a central regulatory axis in the BBB, working through synergistic interactions and distinct functional roles with the Shh and Notch pathways to maintain barrier integrity and functional homeostasis. Notch-Hes serves as a critical upstream regulatory axis of the WNT/β‑catenin pathway, and the two pathways coordinately determine the maturation, tight junction formation, and barrier integrity of the BBB. The Hes family acts as a direct downstream effector of the Notch pathway and a key regulator of the WNT pathway. Hes family transcription factors promote cell cycle progression and stemness by upregulating SOX9, MYC, cyclin D1/D3, while repressing the cell cycle inhibitor p21, thereby promoting the stabilization and nuclear translocation of β‑catenin. Shh is another critical barrier-protective pathway besides the WNT/β‑catenin pathway, activating Gli-1 directly upregulates SOX2 and CCND to control stemness and cell proliferation, and regulates glial markers such as GFAP and S100. Moreover, Shh inhibits GSK3β, a key inhibitory kinase of β‑catenin that directly phosphorylates β‑catenin and targets it for ubiquitination and degradation. Shh:Sonic hedgehog; DKK-1:Dickkopf-related protein 1; LRP5/6:Low-density lipoprotein receptor-related protein 5/6; VEGF:Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor; JAK:Janus Kinase; CK1:Casein Kinase 1; GSK-3:Glycogen Synthase Kinase-3; MYC:N-myc proto-oncogene protein; NICD:Notch intracellular domain; RBP-Jκ:Recombination signal binding protein for immunoglobulin kappa J region; Mt:Mitochondrion; ROS:Reactive Oxygen Species; LEF1: Lymphoid enhancer-binding factor 1; Gli-1:Glioma-associated oncogene 1; GFAP:Glial fibrillary acidic protein; S100:S100 calcium-binding protein; CCND:Cyclin D; Smo:Smoothened protein; RECK:Reversion-inducing cysteine-rich protein with kazal motifs; PLVAP:Plasmalemma vesicle-associated protein; GLUT1:Glucose Transporter 1; LEF1:Lymphoid - enhancer - binding factor 1; ZIC3:Zinc finger protein Zic3; APCDD1:APC down - regulated 1; PI3K: Phosphatidylinositol 3 - kinase; BDNF:Brain - Derived Neurotrophic Factor.

fig.alt-text: Diagram of Wnt/ß-catenin signaling crosstalk, showcasing protein interactions across cell compartments, including receptors, ß-catenin cascades, and downstream effects.
fig.long-desc: This detailed diagram illustrates the Wnt/ß-catenin signaling crosstalk in two panels. Panel A details membrane components—Norrin, Wnt7a/7b, and DKK-1—engaging receptors like LRP5/6 and triggering intracellular ß-catenin cascades. Key proteins such as Axin, APC, and GSK-3 are shown along with SOX17, each playing a role in gene expression regulation. Panel B focuses on the nuclear effects, depicting interactions among NICD, LEF, and RBP-Jk, and includes cellular processes linked to angiogenesis, the blood-brain barrier, and mitochondrial dynamics. The overall layout emphasizes the complexity of molecular interactions and the pathways regulating cellular functions.

caption.p: Role of WNT signaling in BBB dysfunction across neurological diseases.

sec.title: Future perspectives
sec.p: An emerging concept from recent studies is that BBB dysfunction in neurological disease does not arise from uniform loss or gain of WNT signalling, but from spatially and temporally mismatched WNT activity across the cerebrovascular tree. The discussion above highlights the critical role of the WNT signalling pathway in the growth and maintenance of cerebral endothelial cells and its potential as a therapeutic target for disease-related repair. Additionally, several preclinical research strategies targeting selective modulation of the WNT signalling have been developed. Bioengineered WNT ligands may represent a promising direction, as illustrated by recent studies. Researchers have utilized molecules such as WNT7a/b, which regulate BBB formation during embryonic development, and modified them to increase their specificity and efficacy in treating BBB dysfunction [103,111]. Initially, specific mutant forms of WNT7a, such as WNT7aK190A, were created through point mutations. This variant efficiently activates the GPR124/RECK/FZD1 complex without significantly stimulating other Fz receptors. The removal of the C-terminal domain of WNT7a results in the production of a variant called WNT7aNTD, which retains partial activity towards GPR124/RECK/FZD1 but almost does not activate other nontarget receptors, thereby increasing its selectivity for BBB-specific signalling. These bioengineered WNT ligands exemplify a next-generation therapeutic strategy in which BBB repair is achieved through pathway-selective agonism, decoupling beneficial endothelial signalling from off-target WNT activation in non-vascular tissues. Moreover, through large-scale screening of 147 single-residue mutations of WNT7a, specific agonists that exhibit high activity towards GPR124/RECK and low activity towards FZD5 were identified. These engineered WNT ligands demonstrated efficacy in decreasing glioblastoma proliferation and ischaemic stroke infarct sizes in in vitro cellular experiments and in in vivo animal models, and specifically repaired damaged BBBs, suggesting new therapeutic strategies for treating neurodegenerative diseases related to BBB dysfunction [111].

The protective function of the BBB can sometimes impede drug delivery to the brain; thus, the modulation of WNT signalling may be a novel target for increasing BBB permeability. Medulloblastoma is the most common malignant brain tumour in children and, based on gene expression analysis, comprises 4 subtypes with distinct prognoses. Compared with the more treatment-resistant Shh-medulloblastoma subtype (known for its intact BBB), WNT-medulloblastoma (a genotype that includes mutated β-catenin) exhibits an abnormal vascular network, allowing for increased accumulation of chemotherapeutic drugs within the tumour, and improved treatment response. This finding presents a new strategy for enhancing therapeutic efficacy by regulating BBB integrity [112]. The contrast between WNT- and Shh-medulloblastoma further supports the concept that genetically encoded alterations in WNT signalling define tumour-specific BBB states, suggesting that BBB permeability itself can be therapeutically reprogrammed to improve drug efficacy. P-glycoprotein (P-gp) is a critical efflux pump located on the luminal side of the BBB that is responsible for transporting drugs from the brain back into the bloodstream, thereby limiting their entry into the brain. In human BBB cells, the expression of P-gp is regulated by interactions between the classical and non-classical WNT pathways. This interaction involves multiple intracellular transducers, including GSK3 and ras homolog family member A kinase. Research has revealed that the inhibition of ras homolog family member A kinase enhances the phosphorylation and ubiquitination of β-catenin by GSK3, thereby reducing β-catenin-driven P-gp transcriptional activity. This inhibition also increases the transport of P-gp substrates, such as doxorubicin, across the BBB. This provides a new research direction for treating brain diseases, particularly those that require effective drug delivery through the BBB [113]. These findings indicate that WNT signalling governs not only structural components of the BBB, but also its functional transport capacity, positioning WNT as a master regulator of both paracellular and transcellular drug resistance mechanisms.

To gain a deeper understanding of the signalling networks in brain endothelial cells and identify promising molecular targets for endothelial cell-based drug or gene therapies, researchers have integrated multiple signalling pathways, including the VEGF-A, brain-derived neurotrophic factor, nerve growth factor, and WNT, to establish a large-scale computational model. This study demonstrated the potential application of different signalling pathways in disease progression and therapy target prediction in pathological states of the brain, including the WNT signalling pathway. The model was validated through independently established input-output relationships, correctly predicting 73% of the tested scenarios. This comprehensive and powerful computational model not only reveals network interactions that are not apparent in single-pathway studies but also identifies new therapeutic targets for treating severe brain disorders [114]. Integrating WNT signalling into multi-pathway computational frameworks represents a critical step towards predictive BBB biology, enabling the identification of non-intuitive intervention points that are inaccessible through single-pathway analyses. Such systems-level approaches may ultimately guide precision modulation of BBB function for personalized neurotherapeutics. To increase the accuracy of the model, further refinement of the WNT pathway mechanisms through basic research is essential.
